mechanism: Proposed mechanism: Ginger contains pungent phenolic compounds such as gingerols and other constituents studied for anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and GI-related bioactivities; human clinical benefit does not confirm any single mechanism across indications.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference: Folk medicine.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference: Folk medicine.. Folk medicine. Folk medicine
contraindications: Avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding unless supervised by a qualified clinician.
Do not use with known allergy to this plant or related species.
safetyNotes: Generally well tolerated; adverse effects can include heartburn, diarrhea, abdominal discomfort, and mouth/throat irritation; has not been linked to clinically apparent liver injury.
description: The rhizome is the primary part used; commonly consumed as spice and used as a dietary supplement; native to warm parts of Asia and now grown in other regions.
dosage: No single standardized dose is established across indications; clinical trials use product-specific regimens.

preparation: Used as food (fresh/dried) and dietary supplements; rhizome-based products are typical.
region: Native range E. Himalaya to S. Central China, with broader cultivation.
therapeuticUses: Traditional supportive use; indication depends on species part and preparation.
Evidence level varies; use only within established clinical or traditional context.
sideEffects: May cause gastrointestinal upset (nausea, cramping, or diarrhea).
Possible headache, dizziness, or allergic skin reaction in sensitive users.
summary: Ginger rhizome is used in food and supplements; evidence suggests possible benefit for pregnancy-associated nausea/vomiting and menstrual cramps, while results for motion sickness are mostly negative and evidence for osteoarthritis is limited.
